national_context.eez_size_description: Exclusive Economic Zone approximately twice the size of Madagascar's landmass

current_conservation_status_2025_2026.gap_analysis: MPA coverage lags significantly behind terrestrial conservation; 30x30 target requires ~22x increase in marine protected area coverage

policies.policy_name: Law on Fisheries and Aquaculture Code
policies.issuing_body: Ministry of Fisheries and Marine Resources (MPRH)
policies.document_source: Government of Madagascar Official Gazette; The Outlaw Ocean Project
policies.policy_summary: Core fisheries legislation granting MPRH authority to develop and enforce fisheries management and conservation plans. Provides framework for species protection, gear restrictions, marine spatial management, fisher training, and landing site infrastructure requirements.
key_objectives: Regulate fishing and aquaculture activities across Madagascar EEZ
Ensure sustainable resource management and stock conservation
Establish binding conservation plans for fish stocks
Promote fisher training, livelihood development, and technology transfer
Implement marine spatial planning and protected areas
policies.implementation_status: 75% (core legislation active; Decree 2016-1492 provides detailed implementing guidance)
responsible_ministries: Ministry of Fisheries and Marine Resources (MPRH)
Ministry of Environment, Ecology, Forests and the Sea
Local administrative authorities
policies.spatial_scope: Madagascar EEZ (200 nautical miles) + territorial waters (12 nm) and internal waters
policies.temporal_scope: 2015-present (indefinite; subject to amendment via new decrees)

metadata.data_source: Government of Madagascar; The Outlaw Ocean Project; MDPI scholarly review
metadata.notes: Primary fisheries legal framework; actively enforced by MPRH. Decree 2016-1492 provides operational details on fishing gear, size limits, landing requirements, alternative livelihood programs.

policies.document_source: Government of Madagascar Official Gazette; Nairobi Convention Secretariat
policies.policy_summary: Foundational ICZM framework establishing coordinated multi-stakeholder coastal governance across administrative levels and ministries. Mandates integration of environmental, economic, and social planning; cross-ministerial coordination (MPRH, Environment, Local governance); and participatory planning processes.
key_objectives: Establish integrated, multi-sector coastal management approach
Coordinate environmental planning with economic development (fisheries, tourism)
Ensure multi-level governance (national, regional, local) coordination
Integrate climate change adaptation and disaster risk reduction
Support community participation in coastal management
policies.implementation_status: 65% (ICZM strategic frameworks established; implementation and enforcement across administrative levels remains inconsistent)
responsible_ministries: Ministry of Environment, Ecology, Forests and the Sea
Ministry of Fisheries and Marine Resources (MPRH)
Regional & local government authorities
policies.spatial_scope: Madagascar coastal zone (definition varies by region; typically 0-20 km inland + 12 nm seaward)

metadata.data_source: Government of Madagascar; Nairobi Convention Secretariat
metadata.notes: Key implementing instrument for Nairobi Convention ICZM obligations. Policy initiated in 2002, formalized in 2010 decree. Multi-ministerial coordination mechanisms are in place but enforcement consistency varies.

policies.issuing_body: Ministry of Fisheries and Marine Resources
policies.document_source: Government of Madagascar Official Gazette; MDPI scholarly review
policies.policy_summary: Operational decree implementing Law n° 2015-053. Specifies fishing gear restrictions, fish size limits, MPAs/no-take zones, landing site requirements, fisher training obligations, technology transfer provisions, and alternative livelihood programs.
key_objectives: Operationalize fisheries management authority granted in Law 2015-053
Define prohibited fishing gear and methods (e.g., destructive practices)
Establish minimum fish size limits by species and functional group
Specify landing site infrastructure and data collection requirements
Promote fisher training, technology transfer, and aquaculture development
policies.implementation_status: 70% (gear restrictions and size limits enforced; data collection and training implementation varies by region)
responsible_ministries: Ministry of Fisheries and Marine Resources (MPRH)
Port authorities and landing site managers

metadata.data_source: Government of Madagascar; MDPI Governing Distant-Water Fishing article
metadata.notes: Key operational framework for fisheries management. Article 18 (alternative livelihoods) and landing site provisions are critical for data collection supporting ecosystem accounting.

policies.issuing_body: Ministry of Environment, Ecology, Forests and the Sea
policies.document_source: Convention on Biological Diversity (CBD) Secretariat; Government of Madagascar
policies.policy_summary: Comprehensive biodiversity strategy covering terrestrial and marine ecosystems. Establishes goals for protected area creation/management, ecosystem restoration (≥15% degraded area recovery), species conservation, Nagoya Protocol implementation, and integration of climate change adaptation. Marine/coastal ecosystems explicitly covered with USD 203.2M allocated budget.
key_objectives: Expand and effectively manage terrestrial and marine protected areas
Restore at least 15% of degraded ecosystems
Combat desertification and land degradation
Implement Nagoya Protocol for equitable benefit-sharing from genetic resources
Integrate climate change adaptation into biodiversity management
Support sustainable livelihoods for communities dependent on biodiversity
policies.implementation_status: 60% (MPA expansion ongoing; restoration targets partially met; funding gaps persist)
responsible_ministries: Ministry of Environment, Ecology, Forests and the Sea
Ministry of Fisheries and Marine Resources (MPRH)
Regional & local authorities
Community organizations

metadata.data_source: CBD Secretariat; Government of Madagascar
metadata.notes: Strategic framework for biodiversity management. Marine component explicitly includes coral reefs, seagrass, mangroves. Budget allocation (USD 203.2M) and protected area expansion targets directly inform ecosystem accounting scope.

policies.policy_name: Blue Economy Policy (Lettre de Politique Bleue)
policies.issuing_body: Ministry of Fisheries and Marine Resources (MPRH)
policies.policy_summary: Strategic policy outline for sustainable blue economy growth in Madagascar. Balances fisheries sector exploitation with conservation and livelihood objectives. Emphasizes alternative livelihood development, aquaculture promotion, technology transfer, and integration with SDG targets.
key_objectives: Achieve sustainable fisheries and aquaculture development
Increase fisher incomes and livelihood security
Promote alternative livelihoods reducing pressure on wild stocks (aquaculture, ecotourism)
Strengthen enforcement of fisheries regulations
Support community-led marine area management (LMMAs)
policies.implementation_status: 50% (alternative livelihood pilots underway; enforcement remains challenging)
responsible_ministries: Ministry of Fisheries and Marine Resources (MPRH)
Ministry of Economy and Finance
Regional development authorities
policies.spatial_scope: Madagascar EEZ and territorial waters; coastal communities

metadata.data_source: MPRH; MDPI scholarly review; GEF project documentation
metadata.notes: Strategic guidance rather than binding legislation. Shapes MPRH investment priorities. SAPPHIRE project (mud crab aquaculture) and LMMA expansion are key implementation activities.

policies.policy_name: Marine Protected Area Network Expansion Target
policies.issuing_body: Government of Madagascar (World Congress of Parks declaration, 2014)
policies.document_source: Government of Madagascar; World Wildlife Fund
policies.policy_summary: Government commitment to triple the surface area of Madagascar's Marine Protected Areas (MPAs) by 2025, increasing from baseline of ~1.26% EEZ coverage to ~3.78% EEZ coverage. Includes expansion of no-take zones, habitat protection, and community co-management (LMMAs).
key_objectives: Triple MPA coverage by 2025
Increase protection for coral reefs, seagrass, and mangrove ecosystems
Promote community-led marine area management (LMMAs) in parallel with formal MPAs
Enhance coral reef resilience to climate change and fishing pressure
Support food security and livelihood objectives through improved ecosystem health
policies.implementation_status: 45% (22 MPAs now established covering ~1.26% EEZ; target of ~3.78% by 2025 not yet achieved; LMMA network expanding)
responsible_ministries: Ministry of Environment, Ecology, Forests and the Sea
Ministry of Fisheries and Marine Resources (MPRH)
Regional authorities
Community organizations
policies.spatial_scope: Madagascar EEZ (total ~228,000 km²); prioritized in SW Madagascar (Menabe, Androy regions)

metadata.data_source: World Wildlife Fund; Government of Madagascar
metadata.notes: Political commitment at international forum; formalized through NBSAP 2015-2025 and ongoing MPRH programs. Status tracking available through annual MPA inventories (IUCN WDPA database).

policies.document_source: Government of Madagascar Official Gazette; Fisheries Transparency Initiative (FiTI)
policies.policy_summary: Foundational fisheries regulation ordinance (superseded by Law n° 2015-053). Established basic framework requiring fisheries minister to prepare and maintain fisheries management and stock conservation plans.
key_objectives: Regulate fishing and aquaculture activities
Mandate stock conservation planning
Establish fisheries management authority
responsible_ministries: Ministry of Fisheries and Marine Resources (MPRH)

metadata.data_source: Government of Madagascar; FiTI
metadata.notes: Historical legislation; replaced by Law 2015-053. Included for completeness and traceability of legal evolution.

policies.issuing_body: Ministry of Fisheries and Marine Resources (MPRH) + community partners
policies.policy_summary: Community-led marine governance model complementing formal MPAs. Enables local communities to hold management rights over traditional fishing grounds. Involves participatory conservation planning, enforcement by community rangers, and benefit-sharing mechanisms. Often easier to manage and more culturally appropriate than top-down MPAs.
key_objectives: Empower local communities to manage marine resources
Establish community fishing restrictions and enforcement
Ensure equitable benefit-sharing from marine resources
Build local capacity in resource management
Complement formal MPA network with community stewardship
policies.implementation_status: 70% (expanding LMMA network; now ~50+ LMMAs active across Madagascar coast)
responsible_ministries: Ministry of Fisheries and Marine Resources (MPRH)
Local government authorities
Community organizations (Blue Ventures, local fishing communities)
policies.spatial_scope: Coastal communities and fishing grounds across Madagascar; concentrated in SW Madagascar, Ménabe, Androy

metadata.data_source: Blue Ventures; C-3 (Collaborative Conservation); WCS Madagascar
metadata.notes: Innovative governance approach pioneered in Madagascar. Complements formal MPA network and provides data for ecosystem monitoring. Critical for implementation of SEEA services accounting (fisher income, livelihood impacts).

policies.issuing_body: Ministry of Fisheries and the Blue Economy (MPEB) / Ministry of Environment and Sustainable Development (MEDD)
policies.policy_summary: Comprehensive national strategy defining 'Blue Economy' as the optimization of economic, responsible, and sustainable exploitation of maritime and freshwater resources. Operationalized through five strategic axes addressing governance, resource valuation, infrastructure, energy/water access, and climate resilience. Represents a paradigm shift from fragmented sectoral management to integrated ocean governance. Aims to mobilize 100 million USD in sustainable finance by 2030.
key_objectives: Decouple economic growth from environmental degradation in marine sectors
Strengthen cross-sectoral coordination between MPEB and MEDD (resolving previous institutional silos)
Establish the Blue Economy as a macro-economic stabilizer (reducing vulnerability to cyclones, which cost 4.8% annual GDP loss)
Achieve sustainable exploitation of living and non-living marine resources
Enhance food security, energy access, and coastal livelihood resilience
Mobilize 100 million USD in sustainable finance by 2030
policies.implementation_status: 85% (SNEB adopted 2024; detailed implementation roadmap in PNIEB)
responsible_ministries: Ministry of Fisheries and the Blue Economy (MPEB)
Ministry of Environment and Sustainable Development (MEDD)
Ministry of Economy and Finance
Ministry of Infrastructure and Regional Development
policies.spatial_scope: Madagascar EEZ (1,141,120 km²) + high seas adjacent areas + freshwater zones

metadata.data_source: MPEB/MEDD; Government of Madagascar strategic documents
metadata.notes: Represents major institutional restructuring (integration of previously separate MPEB and MEDD mandates). Five strategic axes: (1) Blue Governance Improvement, (2) Natural Resource Valuation, (3) Infrastructure & Service Development, (4) Water & Renewable Energy Access, (5) Climate Resilience & Adaptation.

policies.policy_name: National Investment Plan for the Blue Economy (PNIEB)
policies.issuing_body: Ministry of Fisheries and the Blue Economy (MPEB)
policies.policy_summary: Detailed operational roadmap for implementing SNEB. Specifies capital requirements in hundreds of millions USD for infrastructure development, governance strengthening, and ecosystem restoration. Outlines financing mechanisms including Debt-for-Nature Swaps (DfNS), Blue Bonds, and mobilization of GEF/World Bank/EU/UK funding.
key_objectives: Operationalize the five strategic axes of SNEB
Mobilize hundreds of millions in capital investment
Develop Marine Spatial Planning (PEM) framework
Improve port connectivity and maritime infrastructure
Establish renewable energy projects (marine and hydrological)
Support mangrove restoration and blue carbon projects
policies.implementation_status: 70% (infrastructure projects in pipeline; financing being mobilized)
responsible_ministries: Ministry of Fisheries and the Blue Economy (MPEB)
Ministry of Economy and Finance
Ministry of Infrastructure and Regional Development

metadata.data_source: MPEB; Government of Madagascar
metadata.notes: Key financing mechanisms: Debt-for-Nature Swaps (DfNS), Blue Bonds, GEF Strategic Action Programs, World Bank loans, EU and UK Blue Planet Fund support.

policies.policy_name: National Action Plan for IUU Fishing (PAN-INN) 2025-2029
policies.issuing_body: Ministry of Fisheries and the Blue Economy (MPEB)
policies.document_source: MPEB; SADC MCSCC; Regional Maritime Information Fusion Center (RMIFC) Madagascar
policies.policy_summary: Comprehensive multi-pronged strategy to combat Illegal, Unreported, and Unregulated (IUU) fishing, which costs Madagascar 14.4–23.7 million USD annually. Utilizes satellite monitoring, real-time vessel tracking, beneficial ownership identification, and port state measures. Integrated with SADC Monitoring, Control, and Surveillance Fisheries Coordination Centre (MCSCC) and Regional Maritime Information Fusion Center (RMIFC).
key_objectives: Reduce annual losses from IUU fishing (currently 14.4–23.7 million USD/year)
Establish comprehensive maritime domain awareness through satellite and real-time monitoring
Identify and prevent front companies and politically exposed persons from controlling fishing operations
Implement Port State Measures Agreement (PSMA) enforcement at landing sites
Strengthen intelligence sharing with regional partners (SADC, Seychelles RMIFC)
policies.implementation_status: 75% (satellite monitoring active; PSMA training underway; beneficial ownership framework being operationalized)
responsible_ministries: Ministry of Fisheries and the Blue Economy (MPEB)
Malagasy Navy/Coast Guard
Port authorities
Ministry of Interior (beneficial ownership identification)
policies.spatial_scope: Madagascar EEZ (1,141,120 km²) + adjacent high seas areas + ports

metadata.data_source: MPEB; SADC MCSCC; RMIFC Madagascar
metadata.notes: Multi-institutional approach: integration of RMIFC Madagascar with Regional Centre for Operational Coordination (Seychelles) enables maritime domain awareness. Estimated annual loss: 14.4–23.7 million USD (conservative estimate; actual losses likely higher when accounting for ecological degradation).

policies.document_source: Government of Madagascar Official Gazette; MPEB
policies.policy_summary: Foundational legislation defining Madagascar's maritime zones in accordance with the United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea (UNCLOS). Establishes legal authority for EEZ management (1,141,120 km²), territorial waters (12 nm), contiguous zone, and continental shelf claims. Provides the legal basis for all subsequent ocean governance policies.
key_objectives: Codify Madagascar's maritime zones under international law (UNCLOS)
Establish EEZ management authority (1,141,120 km²)
Define continental shelf claims and extended continental shelf (ECS)
Provide legal framework for resource exploration and conservation
responsible_ministries: Ministry of Fisheries and the Blue Economy (MPEB)
Ministry of Foreign Affairs
policies.spatial_scope: Madagascar maritime zones under UNCLOS (EEZ: 1,141,120 km²)

metadata.data_source: Government of Madagascar; MPEB
metadata.notes: Foundational legislation providing legal basis for all ocean governance. Madagascar's EEZ is approximately twice the size of its landmass.

policies.document_source: Government of Madagascar Official Gazette; MPEB
policies.policy_summary: Establishes the institutional framework for Blue Economy governance, including the creation of the Ministry of Fisheries and the Blue Economy (MPEB). Defines inter-ministerial coordination structures and decision-making processes for integrated ocean governance.
key_objectives: Establish institutional framework for Blue Economy coordination
Create Ministry of Fisheries and the Blue Economy (MPEB)
Define inter-ministerial coordination mechanisms between MPEB and MEDD
Ensure cross-sectoral integration (fisheries, environment, infrastructure, energy)
policies.implementation_status: 90% (MPEB operational; some inter-ministerial coordination challenges remain)
responsible_ministries: Ministry of Fisheries and the Blue Economy (MPEB)
Ministry of Environment and Sustainable Development (MEDD)
All relevant sectoral ministries

metadata.data_source: Government of Madagascar; MPEB
metadata.notes: Key institutional reform: integration of previously fragmented fisheries (MPEB) and environment (MEDD) governance.

policies.policy_name: Decree No. 2015-957 (Decentralization of Ocean Governance & Local Consultation Structures)
policies.document_source: Government of Madagascar Official Gazette; MPEB
policies.policy_summary: Decentralizes ocean governance by creating local consultation structures enabling decentralized territorial collectivities to participate in maritime policy formulation. Provides legal framework for Locally Managed Marine Areas (LMMAs) and traditional community management using 'Dina' (local social codes). Bridges national law with traditional norms through court-ratified restrictions on fishing gear and seasonal closures.
key_objectives: Decentralize ocean governance to local level
Establish local consultation structures for maritime policy
Legitimize Locally Managed Marine Areas (LMMAs)
Integrate traditional community codes (Dina) with national law
Enable community-led enforcement of fishing restrictions and seasonal closures
policies.implementation_status: 75% (LMMA network expanding; Dina legal framework operationalized)
responsible_ministries: Ministry of Fisheries and the Blue Economy (MPEB)
Decentralized territorial collectivities (regional/local government)
Local courts (for Dina ratification)
Community organizations
policies.spatial_scope: Madagascar coastal communities; particularly SW Madagascar (Ménabe, Androy)

metadata.data_source: Government of Madagascar; Blue Ventures; MPEB
metadata.notes: Innovative governance approach: Dina serves as reconciliatory mechanism bridging traditional law and national statutes. LMMA network now includes 50+ operational areas across Madagascar coast.

policies.issuing_body: Ministry of Fisheries and the Blue Economy (MPEB) + Ministry of Environment and Sustainable Development (MEDD)
policies.document_source: MPEB/MEDD; UK Blue Planet Fund; Ocean Country Partnership Programme (OCPP)
policies.policy_summary: Comprehensive revision of marine megafauna protection legislation, supported by OCPP Phase 1 (completed late 2025). Historically, the decree only protected 5 sea turtle species. Updated framework extends full legal protection to 94 marine megafauna species including whales, dolphins, dugongs, threatened sharks/rays, coelacanths, and threatened bony fish based on CITES status.
key_objectives: Expand protection from 5 to 94 marine megafauna species
Provide legal basis for prosecution of wildlife traffickers
Regulate by-catch in industrial and artisanal fisheries
Implement evidence-based management using METT-4 tracking tools
Support Madagascar National Parks (MNP) ranger capacity
policies.implementation_status: 80% (legal update complete; enforcement training and capacity-building ongoing)
responsible_ministries: Ministry of Fisheries and the Blue Economy (MPEB)
Ministry of Environment and Sustainable Development (MEDD)
Madagascar National Parks (MNP)
Fisheries enforcement agencies

metadata.data_source: MPEB/MEDD; OCPP; UK Blue Planet Fund
metadata.notes: Major legislative update: Phase 1 OCPP review (completed late 2025) identified significant gaps and achieved technical consensus on 94-species protection framework. Supported by Seagrass Monitoring Protocol creation and METT-4 training for MNP rangers. Ensures MPAs are not 'paper parks' but functional ecological units.

policies.policy_name: Blue Carbon Initiative — Tahiry Honko Project (Mangrove Conservation)
policies.issuing_body: Ministry of Environment and Sustainable Development (MEDD) + Community Partners + Plan Vivo
policies.document_source: Tahiry Honko Project; Plan Vivo; MEDD
policies.policy_summary: World's largest community-led mangrove carbon conservation project, located in the Bay of Assassins. Preserves 1,200+ hectares of mangroves and generates income through sale of Plan Vivo-verified carbon credits. Demonstrates integration of climate mitigation with community livelihood and coastal protection.
key_objectives: Preserve 1,200+ hectares of intact mangrove forests
Generate sustainable income through voluntary carbon market
Reduce deforestation pressure on mangrove ecosystems
Provide coastal protection and blue carbon storage
Build community capacity in sustainable resource management
responsible_ministries: Ministry of Environment and Sustainable Development (MEDD)
Local community organizations
policies.spatial_scope: Bay of Assassins (Ménabe region), SW Madagascar

metadata.data_source: Tahiry Honko Project; Plan Vivo; MEDD
metadata.notes: World's largest community-led mangrove carbon project. Plan Vivo-verified carbon credits provide direct income to local communities. Model for scaling blue carbon initiatives across Madagascar.

policies.policy_name: Ma Honkô Blue Carbon Initiative (Large-Scale Mangrove Restoration)
policies.issuing_body: Ministry of Environment and Sustainable Development (MEDD) + GEF + World Bank
policies.document_source: MEDD; GEF; World Bank; Scaling Up Ecosystem-Based Adaptation Project
policies.policy_summary: Large-scale mangrove reforestation initiative aiming to restore 40,000 hectares across Sofia, Melaky, Boeny, and Diana regions. Expected to sequester nearly 20 million tCO₂ over project lifetime. Complemented by 'Scaling Up Ecosystem-Based Adaptation' project (launched July 2025, 34 million USD funding) targeting restoration of 3,000 ha mangroves + 2,000 ha degraded watersheds. Integrates nature-based solutions into municipal planning.
key_objectives: Restore 40,000 hectares of mangrove forests
Sequester ~20 million tCO₂ over project lifetime
Integrate climate resilience into local development planning
Support livelihood alternatives (mangrove apiculture, silk production)
Reduce emissions from coastal wetland loss
policies.implementation_status: 65% (restoration projects initiated; 34 million USD Scaling Up EBA project launched July 2025)
responsible_ministries: Ministry of Environment and Sustainable Development (MEDD)
Regional & municipal governments (Boeny, Ménabe, Diana, Atsimo Atsinanana)
policies.spatial_scope: Sofia, Melaky, Boeny, Diana regions; expanded to Atsimo Atsinanana (NW and SW Madagascar)

metadata.data_source: MEDD; GEF; World Bank; Scaling Up EBA Project (July 2025)
metadata.notes: Significant funding milestone: 34 million USD Scaling Up EBA project (July 2025) integrates nature-based solutions into municipal planning in Boeny, Ménabe, Diana, and Atsimo Atsinanana. Expected to remove ~20 million tCO₂ from atmosphere over lifetime.

policies.issuing_body: Ministry of Fisheries and Marine Resources (MPRH)
policies.policy_summary: Declaration prohibiting bottom trawling and large-scale fishing in designated inshore zones to protect coastal ecosystems and small-scale fisher access. Zone extends from coast to ~20 km seaward (varies by region). Aims to protect coral reefs, seagrass, and mangrove juveniles, and ensure small-fisher food security.
key_objectives: Prohibit destructive bottom trawling in coastal zones
Protect coral reef, seagrass, and mangrove ecosystems from bottom-contact gear
Ensure small-scale fishers maintain access to traditional fishing grounds
Reduce fishing mortality on juvenile fish aggregating in nursery habitats
policies.implementation_status: 50% (zone declared and communicated; enforcement by MPRH/coast guard remains inconsistent; illegal trawling still reported)
responsible_ministries: Ministry of Fisheries and Marine Resources (MPRH)
Malagasy Navy/Coast Guard
policies.spatial_scope: Madagascar coastal zone (0-20 km, varies by region); particularly in SW Madagascar (Ménabe, Androy)

metadata.data_source: MPRH; Mongabay investigative reporting; Blue Ventures
metadata.notes: Recent policy with enforcement challenges. Critical for protecting nursery habitats used in ecosystem services accounting (fisheries provisioning, nursery service). Regular monitoring of compliance needed.

international_commitments.commitment_name: Nairobi Convention for the Eastern African Region
international_commitments.document_source: UNEP Regional Seas Programme; Nairobi Convention Secretariat; UN Treaty Collection
international_commitments.commitment_summary: Regional framework for protection and sustainable management of marine and coastal environments in the Eastern African region. Establishes obligations for Integrated Coastal Zone Management (ICZM), Marine Protected Area (MPA) establishment and management, pollution prevention, ecosystem monitoring, and climate change adaptation. Contracting Parties: Comoros, France, Kenya, Madagascar, Mauritius, Mozambique, Seychelles, Somalia, Tanzania, South Africa. COP11 (2026-2027) to be hosted in Antananarivo, Madagascar.
key_obligations: Develop and implement Integrated Coastal Zone Management (ICZM) strategies with cross-ministerial coordination
Establish and manage Marine Protected Areas (MPAs) with clear conservation objectives and enforcement mechanisms
Monitor and control marine pollution (oil spills, waste, nutrient runoff)
Implement Locally Managed Marine Areas (LMMAs) for community-led conservation
Conduct marine ecosystem diagnostic assessments and condition monitoring
Report biennially to Conference of Parties (CoP) on implementation progress
Adopt climate change adaptation measures for coastal resilience
Harmonize fisheries and marine policies across the region (SAPPHIRE project objective)
reporting_requirements: Biennial national reports to Conference of Parties
ICZM strategy updates and implementation status
MPA network inventory and effectiveness assessments
Marine pollution incident reports and response measures
Climate adaptation actions and ecosystem condition monitoring results
international_commitments.regional_context: Western Indian Ocean; Eastern Africa region; 10 Contracting Parties across East Africa, Southern Africa, and island states
international_commitments.enforcement_mechanism: Conference of Parties (CoP) reviews; subsidiary bodies (WIOMSA, scientific committees) monitor compliance; peer reporting and peer review at biennial CoPs

metadata.data_source: UNEP Nairobi Convention Secretariat; UN Treaty Collection; Nairobi Convention CoP reports
metadata.notes: Madagascar a founding/early signatory; strong political commitment. COP11 hosted in Antananarivo demonstrates leadership role. Key framework driving Madagascar's ICZM and MPA policies.

international_commitments.commitment_summary: Global framework for biodiversity conservation. Commits Madagascar to develop national biodiversity strategies, establish protected area networks, restore degraded ecosystems, regulate genetic resource use (Nagoya Protocol), and integrate biodiversity conservation into sectoral planning (agriculture, fisheries, forestry). Madagascar has ratified the Nagoya Protocol and developed NBSAP 2015-2025.
key_obligations: Develop and implement National Biodiversity Strategy and Action Plans (NBSAP)
Establish and manage protected area systems (terrestrial and marine)
Protect and restore degraded habitats (target: 15% restoration by 2025)
Regulate access to genetic resources and fair benefit-sharing (Nagoya Protocol)
Conduct biodiversity assessments and ecosystem monitoring
Report to CBD CoP on progress toward biodiversity targets
Implement ecosystem-based adaptation to climate change
reporting_requirements: National Biodiversity Strategies and Action Plans (NBSAP)
Periodic reports on protected area networks and effectiveness
Nagoya Protocol implementation reports (genetic resource access and benefit-sharing)
Progress toward biodiversity targets (species conservation, habitat restoration, livelihood integration)
international_commitments.regional_context: Global treaty; 196 parties including all African nations
international_commitments.enforcement_mechanism: Conference of Parties (CoP) reviews; scientific advice through Subsidiary Body on Scientific, Technical and Technological Advice (SBSTTA); financial support via Global Environment Facility (GEF)

metadata.data_source: CBD Secretariat; NBSAP 2015-2025
metadata.notes: Madagascar a megadiverse country (endemism hotspot); strong political commitment. NBSAP 2015-2025 is primary implementing instrument for marine/coastal ecosystems.

international_commitments.commitment_name: Convention on International Trade in Endangered Species (CITES)
international_commitments.commitment_summary: International agreement regulating trade in wild species to protect them from unsustainable exploitation. Applies to Madagascar's marine species (sea turtles, whale sharks, clams, corals, seahorses). Madagascar designated as Management Authority and Scientific Authority for species listed in CITES Appendices.
key_obligations: Regulate export/import of CITES-listed species (Appendices I, II, III)
Issue export permits only when scientifically justified and non-detrimental to wild populations
Monitor and report on trade in protected species
Establish Management and Scientific Authorities
Enforce penalties for illegal trade
reporting_requirements: Annual trade reports on CITES-listed species exports/imports
Management of Madagascar-specific marine species (sea turtles, whale sharks, clams, corals)
Enforcement actions against illegal trade
international_commitments.regional_context: Global treaty; 186 parties; Indian Ocean region includes multiple CITES-listed marine species
international_commitments.enforcement_mechanism: CITES Secretariat monitoring; national enforcement (Madagascar customs, MPRH); peer review at CoP meetings

metadata.data_source: CITES Secretariat; Madagascar MPRH
metadata.notes: Madagascar important range state for CITES-listed marine species (sea turtles, whale sharks, clams). Enforcement capacity in enforcement varies; sea turtle poaching remains a challenge.

international_commitments.commitment_summary: Protocol to CBD establishing framework for equitable sharing of benefits from genetic resources. Applies to Madagascar's marine genetic resources (coral reef organisms, seagrass species, mangrove bacteria, fish genomes). Requires prior informed consent (PIC) from Madagascar for access to genetic material and royalty-sharing with local communities.
key_obligations: Establish legal framework for genetic resource access (Prior Informed Consent - PIC)
Negotiate benefit-sharing agreements with researchers/companies accessing Madagascar's genetic resources
Monitor access to genetic resources through checkpoints (research institutions, universities)
Share monetary and non-monetary benefits with local communities
Maintain genetic resource registries/databases
Report on implementation to CBD CoP
reporting_requirements: Annual reports on access to genetic resources and benefit-sharing agreements
Implementation of PIC and Mutually Agreed Terms (MAT)
Distribution of benefits to local communities
international_commitments.regional_context: Global treaty (extension of CBD); currently 135 parties
international_commitments.enforcement_mechanism: CBD CoP Nagoya Protocol subsidiary body; national enforcement through checkpoint system; peer review

metadata.data_source: CBD Secretariat; Government of Madagascar
metadata.notes: Madagascar biodiversity hotspot with high research interest. Implementation framework still developing; local community benefit-sharing mechanisms remain weak in coastal areas.

international_commitments.commitment_name: BBNJ — Agreement on Conservation and Sustainable Use of Marine Biological Diversity of Areas Beyond National Jurisdiction
international_commitments.document_source: UN BBNJ Secretariat; UN Treaty Collection; Government of Madagascar
international_commitments.commitment_summary: Landmark international agreement regulating conservation and sustainable use of marine biodiversity in areas beyond national jurisdiction (high seas). Entry into force January 17, 2026. Provides Madagascar with mandate to participate in establishing high-seas Marine Protected Areas (MPAs), regulating marine genetic resources, and conducting Environmental Impact Assessments (EIAs) for activities in shared waters. Critical for developing nations: includes provisions for capacity-building, technology transfer, and access to marine genetic resources for research.
key_obligations: Participate in establishment of high-seas Marine Protected Areas (MPAs) and area-based management tools
Conduct Environmental Impact Assessments (EIAs) for activities in high seas adjacent to Madagascar's EEZ
Regulate access to and utilization of marine genetic resources beyond national jurisdiction
Implement capacity-building and technology transfer programs
Contribute to monitoring and enforcement of high-seas conservation measures
Report on implementation to BBNJ CoP (Conferences of Parties)
target_ecosystems: High seas areas (pelagic ecosystems beyond Madagascar's EEZ)
Areas ecologically connected to Madagascar's territorial waters
reporting_requirements: Participation in BBNJ CoP meetings (COP1 scheduled 2026)
Capacity-building needs assessments
Reports on marine genetic resource utilization
EIA submissions for activities in adjacent high seas
international_commitments.regional_context: Global high seas governance; Western Indian Ocean pelagic zone
international_commitments.enforcement_mechanism: BBNJ CoP oversight; scientific committees; compliance committee reviews

metadata.data_source: UN BBNJ Secretariat; Government of Madagascar; UN Treaty Collection
metadata.notes: CRITICAL MILESTONE: Entry into force January 17, 2026 is a watershed moment for Madagascar's ocean governance. Madagascar signed Sept 25, 2024; ratified Sept 23, 2025. Provides developing-nation protections for capacity-building and technology transfer. COP1 participation scheduled for 2026.

international_commitments.document_source: Nairobi Convention Secretariat; Government of Madagascar; UN Environment
international_commitments.commitment_summary: Standardized legal framework for Integrated Coastal Zone Management (ICZM) adopted during Conference of Plenipotentiaries in Antananarivo, September 2023. Adopted by 10 signatory countries. Provides coordinated regional approach to transboundary issues: climate change vulnerability, ocean acidification, marine pollution, and disaster risk reduction.
key_obligations: Implement ICZM strategies based on Climate Change Vulnerability Assessment (CCVA) toolkit
Identify vulnerable communities using standardized vulnerability criteria
Monitor ocean acidification impacts (especially on coral reefs and mollusks)
Coordinate marine pollution response (oil spill preparedness following 2020 Mauritius lesson)
Integrate disaster risk management into coastal planning
Share data and best practices with regional partners
reporting_requirements: CCVA updates and vulnerability assessments
Ocean acidification monitoring data
Marine pollution incident reports and response actions
ICZM strategy implementation reports
international_commitments.regional_context: Western Indian Ocean; Eastern & Southern Africa region (10 Contracting Parties)
international_commitments.enforcement_mechanism: Nairobi Convention CoP oversight; regional coordination committee

metadata.data_source: Nairobi Convention Secretariat; Government of Madagascar
metadata.notes: Madagascar hosted CoP10 in 2021 and led ICZM Protocol negotiation. Adopted September 2023 in Antananarivo. Regional Ocean Acidification Action Plan adopted separately in 2022. Madagascar chairs task forces on coral reefs and mangroves.

international_commitments.commitment_name: Regional Ocean Acidification Action Plan (ROAAP) — Nairobi Convention
international_commitments.commitment_summary: Regional action plan specifically addressing ocean acidification impacts in the Western Indian Ocean. Focuses on monitoring pH changes in coral reef and mollusk habitats, early warning systems, and adaptation strategies. Adopted 2022 under Nairobi Convention.
key_obligations: Establish and maintain ocean acidification monitoring stations
Monitor pH and carbonate chemistry in coral reef and seagrass ecosystems
Develop early warning systems for acidification events
Support research on acidification impacts on local species
Share monitoring data with regional partners
reporting_requirements: Annual ocean acidification monitoring data
Research reports on species-specific impacts
Early warning system performance reports
international_commitments.regional_context: Western Indian Ocean; coordinated regional monitoring
international_commitments.enforcement_mechanism: Nairobi Convention CoP oversight; scientific working group

metadata.data_source: Nairobi Convention Secretariat; UNEP
metadata.notes: Adopted 2022 to address emerging threat of ocean acidification. Madagascar's coral reef and mollusk sectors particularly vulnerable to pH changes.

international_commitments.commitment_name: ILO Just Transition Framework (Applied to Blue Economy)
international_commitments.commitment_summary: ILO's 'Just Transition' framework applied to Madagascar's blue economy to ensure equitable inclusion of vulnerable workers (fishers, women, youth) in transition toward sustainable sectors. Pioneered in lobster-catching sector (Southern Madagascar) through inter-professionalization (bringing together fishers, processors, exporters) to formalize labor relations and improve social dialogue.
key_obligations: Ensure equitable transition from depleted/destructive fishing to sustainable alternatives
Support labor formalization and decent work standards
Facilitate inter-professionalization (fisher-processor-exporter dialogue)
Provide vocational training for emerging sectors (aquaculture, ecotourism)
Ensure gender equity and youth inclusion in alternative livelihoods
Monitor socio-economic outcomes (Supply and Use Tables, Green Jobs Assessment Models)
reporting_requirements: Supply and Use Tables (SUT) for blue economy sectors
Green Jobs Assessment Models (GJAM) results
Labor formalization metrics
Training outcomes for women and youth
international_commitments.regional_context: Madagascar blue economy; Western Indian Ocean
international_commitments.enforcement_mechanism: ILO technical support; national social dialogue processes

metadata.data_source: ILO; Government of Madagascar; MPEB
metadata.notes: Applied framework (not binding treaty). Pioneered in Southern Madagascar lobster sector. Utilizes SUT and GJAM tools to estimate job impacts of climate policies and identify vocational training needs.

international_commitments.commitment_name: Western Indian Ocean Large Marine Ecosystem (WIO LME) Strategic Action Programme (SAPPHIRE)
international_commitments.organization: Indian Ocean Commission (IOC) / GEF / World Bank
international_commitments.commitment_summary: Regional program coordinating marine policy harmonization and institutional reforms across 8 WIO countries (Comoros, Kenya, Madagascar, Mauritius, Mozambique, Seychelles, South Africa, Tanzania). Pilot alternative livelihoods (e.g., mud crab and mussel aquaculture in Madagascar) to reduce pressure on wild fish stocks. Supports ecosystem-based management and community livelihood integration.
key_obligations: Harmonize national marine policies with regional standards
Support ecosystem-based management practices
Develop alternative livelihood programs (aquaculture, ecotourism, marine biotechnology)
Strengthen institutional capacity for regional cooperation
Monitor ecosystem health and socioeconomic outcomes
reporting_requirements: National progress reports on policy harmonization
Alternative livelihood pilot implementation status
Ecosystem monitoring data
Livelihood outcome metrics (fisher income, food security, gender inclusion)
international_commitments.enforcement_mechanism: Regional steering committee; IOC secretariat coordination; GEF monitoring and evaluation

metadata.data_source: Nairobi Convention Secretariat; IOC; GEF project documents
metadata.notes: Active regional program with Madagascar leading mud crab aquaculture pilot in Ménabe. Directly supports ecosystem services accounting (livelihood integration, alternative income pathways).

policy_commitment_linkages.implementation_gap: ICZM Decree (2010-137) partially implements Nairobi Convention ICZM obligations. Coastal zone mapping and multi-sector coordination frameworks are in place, but enforcement across administrative levels remains inconsistent. Cross-ministerial coordination committees established but enforcement authority limited at local level.
policy_commitment_linkages.cross_reference_section: Nairobi Convention Article 3 (ICZM); Madagascar Decree N° 2010-137 Articles 1-15
policy_commitment_linkages.notes: Madagascar's ICZM framework was explicitly developed to meet Nairobi Convention obligations initiated in 2002. Regular biennial reporting to CoP occurs.

policy_commitment_linkages.implementation_gap: NBSAP 2015-2025 fully operationalizes CBD commitments. Primary gap: funding shortfall (USD 203.2M budget vs. ~USD 100M mobilized). MPA expansion target of 3.78% EEZ by 2025 is behind schedule (~1.26% achieved as of 2026).
policy_commitment_linkages.cross_reference_section: CBD Articles 5-7 (protected areas, monitoring); NBSAP 2015-2025 Implementation Strategy
policy_commitment_linkages.notes: NBSAP is Madagascar's primary instrument for CBD implementation. Scheduled for update in 2025 toward post-2020 Global Biodiversity Framework.

policy_commitment_linkages.implementation_gap: Fisheries Law grants MPRH authority to regulate CITES-listed species, but enforcement mechanisms remain weak. Illegal sea turtle and clam trade continues despite restrictions; enforcement by coast guard limited by capacity and resources.
policy_commitment_linkages.cross_reference_section: Law n° 2015-053 Articles 18-25 (species protection); CITES Appendices (sea turtles, whale sharks, clams, corals)
policy_commitment_linkages.notes: CITES implementation delegated to MPRH; actual enforcement capacity constrained. Sea turtle poaching in Ménabe region remains significant challenge.

policy_commitment_linkages.implementation_gap: MPA expansion target (triple by 2025) is anchored in Nairobi Convention obligations. Progress is 45% toward target. Key gap: effective management and enforcement in newly designated MPAs; funding for ranger networks and monitoring.
policy_commitment_linkages.cross_reference_section: Nairobi Convention Articles 2-5 (marine protection); 2014 World Parks Sydney Declaration commitment; NBSAP MPA targets
policy_commitment_linkages.notes: MPA network expansion is visible commitment to Nairobi Convention. Effectiveness monitoring required to demonstrate biodiversity outcomes.

policy_commitment_linkages.implementation_gap: LMMA framework complements formal MPA network (Nairobi Convention objective). However, legal basis for LMMAs remains unclear; community management rights are recognized informally but not codified in national law. Benefit-sharing mechanisms remain underdeveloped.
policy_commitment_linkages.cross_reference_section: Nairobi Convention Article 4 (community participation); Decree 2010-137 (ICZM multi-stakeholder approach); Law 2015-053 (LMMA recognition as conservation tool)
policy_commitment_linkages.notes: LMMAs are innovative approach pioneered in Madagascar. Increasingly recognized as effective co-management tool; requires legal codification to ensure long-term sustainability.

policy_commitment_linkages.implementation_gap: Blue Economy policy aligns with SAPPHIRE objective of alternative livelihoods (aquaculture, ecotourism). Implementation gap: alternative livelihood pilots are limited in scale and coverage; most coastal communities still dependent on wild fish stocks.
policy_commitment_linkages.cross_reference_section: Blue Policy 2015 Articles 5-8 (alternative livelihoods); SAPPHIRE project documentation (mud crab aquaculture targets)
policy_commitment_linkages.notes: SAPPHIRE mud crab pilot in Ménabe is primary implementation activity. Scale-up to other regions needed to reduce pressure on wild stocks.

policy_commitment_linkages.implementation_gap: Law No. 2018-025 provides foundational legal framework for BBNJ implementation. Gap: Madagascar must develop operational capacity (high-seas MPA nomination process, EIA procedures, marine genetic resource regulations) to fully implement BBNJ obligations. Capacity-building under BBNJ provisions will support this.
policy_commitment_linkages.cross_reference_section: Law No. 2018-025 (EEZ/continental shelf definition); BBNJ Agreement (Articles on MPAs, EIAs, marine genetic resources)
policy_commitment_linkages.notes: Critical: BBNJ entry into force (January 17, 2026) requires Madagascar to activate high-seas governance mechanisms. COP1 participation scheduled 2026. Capacity-building provisions essential for developing-nation implementation.

policy_commitment_linkages.implementation_gap: ICZM Decree (2010-137) directly implements Nairobi Convention ICZM Protocol (adopted Sept 2023). High alignment. Minor gap: CCVA toolkit implementation in all coastal communities remains incomplete; vulnerability assessment data not yet available for all regions.
policy_commitment_linkages.cross_reference_section: Decree 2010-137 (ICZM framework); Nairobi Convention ICZM Protocol Articles 1-5
policy_commitment_linkages.notes: Madagascar hosted Nairobi Convention COP10 (2021) and led ICZM Protocol negotiation. Protocol adopted Antananarivo Sept 2023. Strong institutional alignment.

policy_commitment_linkages.implementation_gap: SNEB's governance improvement axis (Axe 1) supports BBNJ capacity-building needs. Gap: specific operational procedures for high-seas MPA nomination, EIA review, and marine genetic resource management not yet codified in SNEB implementation plan.
policy_commitment_linkages.cross_reference_section: SNEB (Axe 1: Blue Governance Improvement); BBNJ Agreement (Capacity-building provisions)
policy_commitment_linkages.notes: SNEB provides overarching governance framework that will enable BBNJ implementation. Operational procedures to be developed in PNIEB detailed workplan.

policy_commitment_linkages.implementation_gap: PAN-INN (2025-2029) directly implements Nairobi Convention mandate to combat IUU fishing. High alignment on objectives. Gap: enforcement capacity in remote areas remains limited; satellite monitoring integration with regional partners (RMIFC) still being operationalized.
policy_commitment_linkages.cross_reference_section: PAN-INN 2025-2029 (multi-pronged approach); Nairobi Convention Article 7 (Fisheries protection)
policy_commitment_linkages.notes: Regional Maritime Information Fusion Center (RMIFC) Madagascar operational; integration with Seychelles Regional Centre for Operational Coordination provides sophisticated maritime domain awareness.

policy_commitment_linkages.implementation_gap: Tahiry Honko mangrove carbon project supports Nairobi Convention climate resilience objectives. Gap: integration of blue carbon benefits into formal ICZM coastal planning frameworks still developing.
policy_commitment_linkages.cross_reference_section: Tahiry Honko Project documentation; Nairobi Convention ICZM Protocol (Climate adaptation component)
policy_commitment_linkages.notes: World's largest community-led mangrove carbon project. Plan Vivo-verified carbon credits provide direct income to local communities.

policy_commitment_linkages.implementation_gap: SNEB's socio-economic integration (Axe 2: Natural Resource Valuation) incorporates Just Transition principles. Gap: comprehensive formalization of fishing sector not yet achieved; inter-professionalization models only piloted in lobster sector.
policy_commitment_linkages.cross_reference_section: SNEB Axe 2 (livelihood integration); ILO Just Transition Framework
policy_commitment_linkages.notes: ILO pioneered lobster-sector interventions demonstrating formalization model. Supply and Use Tables (SUT) and Green Jobs Assessment Models (GJAM) tools available for monitoring.

ecosystem_coverage.spatial_extent: ~1,400 km of reef distributed along Madagascar coast; concentrated in SW Madagascar (Ménabe, Androy regions); 22 MPAs providing ~1.26% EEZ coverage (target: 3.78% by 2025)
Distributed along Madagascar coast, particularly in coastal bays and estuaries; seagrass-watch monitoring program active
ecosystem_coverage.conservation_status: VULNERABLE (limited data; presumed degraded by trawling and nutrient runoff)

ecosystem_coverage.spatial_extent: ~250,000 ha nationwide (2% of global mangrove coverage); concentrated in NW Madagascar (Ménabe, Melaky regions); Sakueta, Nossi-Bé priority conservation areas
ecosystem_coverage.conservation_status: DEGRADED (ongoing deforestation from charcoal production; restoration programs expanding)
key_threats: Charcoal production (~500 ha/year loss in NW Madagascar)
Climate change impacts (sea-level rise, salinity changes)
